actions.label: Do the Work Yourself
actions.description: You are the business. Every client, every deliverable, every email. It's unsustainable, but it's how you learn.
actions.narrative_success: Everything got done. Everything. You're exhausted, but every client is happy — because you did it all personally.
actions.narrative_failure: You dropped a ball. One client got a late delivery. Nothing catastrophic, but a reminder that one person can't do everything.

actions.label: Hire Your First Contractor
actions.description: Offload delivery work to someone else for the first time. Terrifying. Necessary.
actions.narrative_success: She's not as good as you. She's 80% as good, and that 80% just bought you back twenty hours a week.
actions.narrative_failure: The contractor needs more hand-holding than expected. You're spending time managing instead of doing — but that's the transition.

actions.label: Document Your First SOP
actions.description: Write down how you actually do the work. Step by step. So someone else could follow it without calling you.
actions.narrative_success: It took twelve hours to write what you do in your sleep. But now it exists outside your head for the first time.
actions.narrative_failure: The SOP is written but it's incomplete. You realize how much of your process is instinct, not system.

actions.label: Build Quality Control
actions.description: Create a checklist and review process so work goes out right the first time, even when you're not looking.
actions.narrative_success: Zero client complaints this month. The checklist caught two errors before they went out the door.
actions.narrative_failure: The checklist exists. People are using it... sometimes. Consistency will take another month of enforcement.

actions.label: Build Client Onboarding
actions.description: Create a smooth intake process — contracts, expectations, kickoff. Stop winging the first week with every new client.
actions.narrative_success: New clients now get a welcome packet, a signed scope, and a kickoff call. You look like a real company now.
actions.narrative_failure: The onboarding flow is built but clunky. Half the clients still have questions the packet should have answered.

actions.label: Automate Repetitive Tasks
actions.description: Set up email templates, invoice automation, scheduling tools. Reclaim hours from admin busywork.
actions.narrative_success: Invoices go out automatically now. Scheduling is self-serve. You just got back five hours a week you didn't know you were losing.
actions.narrative_failure: The automation is partially working. Invoices are automated; scheduling still needs manual intervention.

actions.label: Build a Delivery Team
actions.description: Hire two to three people dedicated to fulfillment. You manage them. They do the work. The business scales.
actions.narrative_success: The team handled this month's workload without a single escalation to you. You built something that works without you.
actions.narrative_failure: The team is hired and training. Quality isn't where you want it yet. You're managing more than doing — the awkward middle phase.

actions.label: Implement Project Management
actions.description: Deploy real project tracking — Asana, Monday, or similar. Everyone sees what's due, who owns it, and what's late.
actions.narrative_success: For the first time, you can see every active project, every deadline, every bottleneck — in one screen. This changes everything.
actions.narrative_failure: The tool is set up. Adoption is mixed. Your best contractor refuses to update her tasks. People problems, not software problems.

actions.label: Build a Hiring Pipeline
actions.description: Stop panic-hiring. Build a repeatable process for finding, vetting, and onboarding new team members.
actions.narrative_success: You have a job post template, an interview script, and a 30-day onboarding plan. Next hire takes days to onboard, not weeks.
actions.narrative_failure: The pipeline exists on paper. In practice, you're still gut-checking candidates over coffee. But the structure is started.

actions.label: Hire a Manager
actions.description: Promote or hire someone to manage the team so you manage one person instead of five.
actions.narrative_success: She ran the Monday meeting without you this week. The team didn't even notice you weren't there. That's the goal.
actions.narrative_failure: The new manager is learning. Team dynamics shifted. Two people aren't happy about the new layer. Growing pains.

actions.label: Build Fulfillment System
actions.description: Systematize delivery end-to-end: intake, production, QA, delivery, follow-up. All documented, all tracked.
actions.narrative_success: A new client moves through your system like a product on an assembly line — consistent, reliable, scalable. This is a real business now.
actions.narrative_failure: The system is built for 80% of cases. The other 20% still need you. But that's 80% fewer fires.

actions.label: Full Systemization
actions.description: The business runs without you. SOPs for everything. Manager handles daily ops. You're the owner, not the operator.
actions.narrative_success: You took a week off. Revenue was the same when you came back. This is what freedom looks like.
actions.narrative_failure: Almost. Two client issues escalated to you during your 'vacation.' Close, but the safety net has holes.

actions.label: Multi-Market Expansion
actions.description: Open a second location or market. Clone what works into a new geography or vertical.
actions.narrative_success: Location two is open and profitable in month three. The playbook translated. You're an empire now.
actions.narrative_failure: The new market is slower than expected. Overhead is real. It'll take six months to break even — if it does.

actions.label: Build Proprietary IP
actions.description: Develop proprietary tools, methodology, or technology that competitors can't replicate. Your moat.
actions.narrative_success: The proprietary framework is built and branded. Clients now come to you for the methodology, not just the service.
actions.narrative_failure: The IP is developed but not yet differentiated enough. More iteration needed. Still, the foundation is laid.

actions.label: Vertical Integration
actions.description: Bring a supplier or distribution channel in-house. Control more of the value chain, keep more margin.
actions.narrative_success: You own the supply chain now. Margins jumped. Competitors are paying retail for what you produce in-house.
actions.narrative_failure: The integration is messier than expected. You're saving on COGS but spending more on management overhead.
